metadata.summary: Privacy-by-design voice interfaces: consent disclosure, trust indicators, step-up auth, and PII redaction. Voice data is persistent; privacy must be designed from the first dialog.

content.title: VUI Privacy and Security
content.summary: Voice interfaces handling personal data require privacy-by-design: transparency before capture, visual plus audio trust indicators, step-up authentication for sensitive actions, and PII redaction at transcript boundaries. Voice data is legally regulated (GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, BIPA) and technically leaky; cloud transcripts persist on third-party infrastructure.

why.p: Voice capture is legally regulated (GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, BIPA) and technically leaky: cloud transcripts persist on third-party infrastructure, LLM context crosses turns, and "private mode" UI cannot prevent model-level logging. Privacy-by-design prevents post-launch regulatory exposure and user trust failures that are expensive to remediate.

Visual indicators alone exclude blind users; audio-only excludes deaf users. Both channels together cover the full user range and build consistent trust. Hardware kill switches (mute buttons) are essential fallbacks when software stop commands fail.

list.item: Designing a voice agent that handles personal data (banking, health, identity, child profiles).
Submitting an Alexa/Google Action for certification — privacy disclosures are mandatory and reviewed.
Operating in regulated jurisdictions: GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA/CPRA, Illinois BIPA, EU AI Act (voice biometrics).
Building voice flows in customer support that may capture card numbers, OTPs, or account secrets.
Internal-only voice prototypes with synthetic data — full disclosure flows can be deferred until external pilot.
Non-voice UI forms — apply standard data-minimisation principles instead; voice-specific patterns do not apply.

rule.statement: Disclose data collection at the start of any session that captures voice data.
rule.rationale: GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, and BIPA require informed consent before biometric or health-related voice capture. Disclosure at session start prevents retroactive consent failures.

rule.statement: Collect only the voice data strictly required for the current task; do not retain beyond the session unless the user explicitly consents.
rule.rationale: Data minimization limits regulatory exposure and reduces the blast radius of a transcript breach.

rule.statement: Provide a "stop listening" command that is always available and honored within one utterance, backed by a hardware mute when possible.
rule.rationale: Software-only stop commands fail when ASR is degraded. A hardware kill switch guarantees the user can always revoke capture regardless of model state.

rule.statement: Allow users to access and delete their voice history at any time through a discoverable interface.
rule.rationale: GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) and CCPA deletion rights are non-negotiable in production deployments.

rule.statement: Show a visual indicator (light, icon) AND play an audio confirmation whenever the microphone is active.
rule.rationale: Visual-only indicators exclude blind users; audio-only excludes deaf users. Both channels together cover the full user range and build consistent trust.

example.note: LED ring glows amber during listening plus chime plays on activation. "Stop listening" command tested and confirmed to silence both within 500ms.
Visual waveform animation only while listening. Blind users have no way to know capture is active; no hardware mute fallback.

section.name: sensitive-operations
section.title: Sensitive Operations

rule.statement: Require step-up authentication (OTP, TOTP, biometric factor) before executing any destructive, financial, or identity-changing action via voice.
rule.rationale: Voice authentication alone is too weak for high-value actions: voice cloning is accessible and speaker ID fails under noise. Step-up authentication adds a knowledge or possession factor.

rule.statement: Never repeat back full sensitive values the user provided; mask to last 4 digits or equivalent when confirming.
rule.rationale: Repeating "your card number 4111 1111 1111 1111" in a shared space leaks the full number. Masking "the card ending 1111" preserves confirmation without exposure.

rule.statement: Redact PII at the tool-call boundary, before any logging or persistence layer receives the data.
rule.rationale: Tool-call payloads are often logged with full fidelity. Redacting at the boundary — not after persistence — is the only reliable prevention.

section.reference: Disclosure emitter and regex PII redactor for GDPR/HIPAA jurisdictions

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.title: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Build a three-subagent privacy pipeline: the first reviews dialog flows for PII capture and missing consent; the second drafts jurisdiction-compliant disclosure scripts; the third runs adversarial replay (jailbreaks, social engineering) against the deployed agent. All three outputs require human-in-the-loop: legal review of disclosure copy and DPIA sign-off before launch.

rule.statement: Require legal review for all disclosure scripts and security review for all transcript-handling pipelines before production deployment.
rule.rationale: Automated agents cannot assess legal sufficiency of consent language. Disclosure that fails GDPR Article 13 or HIPAA Notice of Privacy Practices requirements creates direct regulatory liability.

example.note: Privacy audit prompt: "You are a VUI privacy auditor. For each dialog turn, output JSON: { turn, captures_pii: bool, pii_types[], consent_present: bool, sensitive_action: bool, recommendation }. PII types: name, email, phone, address, dob, ssn, card, health, biometric, location, child. If sensitive_action true (transfer, delete, change-password), require step-up auth in recommendation."

antipattern.description: Embedding real user PII or API secrets in the system prompt, treating it as private.
antipattern.reason: The system prompt is semi-public — adversaries extract it via creative jailbreaks. Any secrets or PII embedded in prompts will be leaked eventually.

antipattern.description: Assuming "delete from our database" removes voice data from all persistence layers.
antipattern.reason: Cloud transcripts persist on third-party infrastructure (OpenAI, Google, AWS). Local deletion does not guarantee removal from provider storage unless Zero Data Retention is explicitly negotiated and documented in the DPIA.

antipattern.description: Trusting voice speaker ID as sufficient authentication for high-value actions.
antipattern.reason: Voice cloning is accessible and speaker ID fails under noise. Adversaries can bypass voice biometrics with clones; step-up with a knowledge or possession factor is mandatory for high-risk actions.

antipattern.description: Logging tool-call payloads without redacting PII at the boundary.
antipattern.reason: Tool calls typically log request and response payloads in full. PII redaction must happen at the tool boundary before any persistence layer receives the data — not as a post-processing step.

list.item: GDPR: ICO guidance on voice-data and consent — https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/
HIPAA Security Rule — https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/security/index.html
Illinois BIPA (biometric voice prints): 740 ILCS 14
EU AI Act voice biometrics provisions — https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689
OWASP LLM Top 10 — https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/

commands.command: PII detection/redaction in transcripts. `pip install presidio-analyzer presidio-anonymizer`
Encrypt voice transcripts at rest. `pip install cryptography`
Manage keys for encrypted voice payloads. cloud SDKs
LLM red-team scanner — works on voice transcripts. `pip install garak`
Detect secrets in committed prompts/configs. `pip install trufflehog`

item.term: SaaS, required for HIPAA/GDPR-sensitive prompts; opt-in via API.
SaaS, step-up auth (TOTP, push) for sensitive operations.
SaaS, voice biometrics with explicit consent flows.
OSS dataset, diverse training data, transparent licensing.
OSS, keep transcripts on-prem; no third-party dependency.

section.name: pii-redaction-template
section.title: PII Redaction Template (Python)

list.item: Treat the system prompt as semi-public — adversaries will extract it. Never embed real user PII or API secrets in it.
Use ephemeral session IDs and rotate; never tie voice IDs to permanent user IDs without separate consent.
Mask spoken back any data the user provided ("...the card ending 4321") — never re-read full numbers.
Default to opt-out for voice-print biometrics; require explicit verbal plus written consent under BIPA-equivalent laws.
Implement step-up auth (OTP, TOTP, biometric) before any destructive or financial action — voice alone is too weak.
LLMs leak prior conversation context across turns; isolate user sessions explicitly and drop history at session end.
Realtime API stores audio for abuse monitoring by default; enable Zero Data Retention for sensitive deployments and document it in the DPIA.
Tool-call payloads often include unredacted PII in logs; redact at the tool boundary, not after persistence.
"Private mode" prompts ("don't log this") cannot be enforced — always assume model and provider see everything.
Adversaries use voice cloning to bypass speaker ID; require liveness or knowledge-factor for high-value actions.
Human checkpoint: legal review for disclosure scripts; security review for transcript-handling pipeline; periodic red-team replay.

template.purpose: Disclosure emitter and regex PII redactor for GDPR/HIPAA jurisdictions

see-also.link: ICO guidance on voice-data and consent
EU AI Act voice biometrics provisions
